title: Numbers & Everyday Info Overview
subtitle: Listen to the lesson explained in English

transcript.en: Hey! 👋 Unit 2. Numbers, prices, months — all the everyday info you need.
Four things today: **counting 1 to 30**, the **tens up to 100**, **money and age**, and the **twelve months**. Plus a grammar twist that surprises most learners. Let's go!
The scene: you're at a pastizzi counter. Pastizzi — those flaky savoury pastries you'll smell everywhere. How much is one?
How much is this pastizz? The reply:
Thirty cents. And if you want two?
Note the special form of 'two' used before a noun. You'll meet that pattern in the grammar section. 😊
Here they are. Thanks! Three words, zero stress. ☕
Let's count. 1 to 10 first — these are the building blocks for everything else.
The teens are 11 to 19. They follow a 'stacked' pattern in Maltese — 11, 12, 13 sound nothing like English but are very consistent once you hear them a few times.
twenty. From 21 onwards you say the unit first, then 'u' (and), then the ten:
twenty-one. Same pattern right up to:
All the tens from 40 to 100. Once you have these, you can say any number up to 100 using the same 'unit + u + ten' trick.
Same pattern every time. One formula, infinite numbers. 🎯
The first word you'll need at any till — money:
Now, age. The Maltese way to ask someone's age literally asks 'how much time do you have?':
Fun, right? And to answer — I'm thirty years old:
The verb here means 'I have'. Maltese says 'I have thirty years', not 'I am thirty'. 😄
The twelve months. Most look like their English or Italian cousins, so these come fairly quickly. 🗓️
📖 PART 6 — GRAMMAR: HOW NUMBERS BEND THE NOUN 📖
Here's the one thing that surprises every English speaker. Numbers in Maltese **change the noun after them**. Three gears.
GEAR 1: 'two' before a noun uses a special shortened form — not the plain word for two. So two books:
GEAR 2: numbers 3 to 10 slim down and the noun goes **plural**. Three books:
GEAR 3: from 11 upwards the noun flips back to **singular**. Eleven years:
Compare: ten books uses the plural noun. Twenty-three books flips back to singular. Two different gears! English never does this, so give yourself time. 🎯
Ordinals — first, second, third — always travel with 'the':
Numbers 1 to 100. Money. Age. Twelve months. And the three-gear counting rule. That's Unit 2.
Pick any section and let the exercises do the drilling. You're building fast. 💪
